events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is a never-smoker with no alcohol use history, of West African descent, works as a high school music teacher. She has a history of monogamous partnership and remote HPV exposure. She has no significant past medical history other than well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. She has no family history of head and neck cancers.

At age 52 years, the patient was diagnosed with stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition p16-positive oropharyngeal group: stage I by HPV-positive staging criteria) squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue (ICD-O site C01.9, histology code 8070/3: squamous cell carcinoma, NOS). The tumor measured 3.2 cm at the base of tongue with a single ipsilateral level II lymph node measuring 2.1 cm, no distant metastases. The tumor was p16 positive (surrogate for HPV), confirming HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Relevant biomarkers: p16 IHC strongly positive (3+, >75% of cells), HPV in situ hybridization positive for high-risk HPV (subtype 16), EBER negative. PD-L1 CPS score: 12. Tumor confirmation: positive histology via direct laryngoscopy biopsy. Smoking history: never smoker (0 pack-years). ECOG performance status: 0. The patient initially presented with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Oropharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic, friable mass at the right base of tongue with right level II lymphadenopathy.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of the right base of tongue mass. Pathology showed invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated (grade 2), arising from the base of tongue mucosa with keratinization and intercellular bridges. Immunohistochemistry: p16 diffusely and strongly positive (block-like nuclear and cytoplasmic staining in >75% of tumor cells), consistent with transcriptionally active HPV infection. CK5/6 positive, p63 positive, CK7 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 65%. HPV RNA ISH performed concurrently confirmed high-risk HPV type 16 RNA expression in tumor cells. Pathologic margins: not applicable (biopsy). Fine needle aspiration of right level II lymph node: positive for metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent an MRI of the neck with and without contrast for staging. MRI demonstrated a 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing mass centered at the right base of tongue with extension toward the right tonsillar fossa but without extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space. There was a single right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm with internal necrosis and peripheral enhancement, consistent with metastatic adenopathy. No bilateral or contralateral adenopathy identified. No evidence of retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy. No bony involvement of the mandible or skull base. No perineural invasion identified on imaging. Overall impression: T2N1M0, stage III (AJCC 8th ed. conventional), stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent PET-CT (18F-FDG) for staging. PET-CT confirmed an FDG-avid mass at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 14.2) with a single FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 9.8, measuring 2.1 cm). No contralateral cervical adenopathy. No mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular nodal disease. No distant metastases. Lungs: clear, no nodules. Liver, spleen: unremarkable. Impression: FDG-avid right base of tongue primary with single right-sided regional nodal metastasis. Consistent with stage III (T2N1M0) oropharyngeal SCC, favorable HPV-positive subtype.

At age 52 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed on tumor biopsy tissue from the right base of tongue using a comprehensive solid tumor panel (523-gene panel). Results: PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (allele frequency 18%, pathogenic, actionable); TERT promoter C228T mutation (allele frequency 22%, pathogenic); FAT1 p.Q2401* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 15%, likely pathogenic); EP300 p.R1627Q missense mutation (allele frequency 11%, variant of uncertain significance); NOTCH1 p.A465T missense mutation (allele frequency 9%, variant of uncertain significance). Copy number alterations: CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, EGFR copy number gain (low-level, 3.1 copies). No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, or TP53 mutations detected. No actionable fusions (ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, FGFR2/3 all negative). MSI status: microsatellite stable (MSS). TMB: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 (SP142): CPS 12. No EGFR exon 19/21 activating mutations. HPV integration detected. Germline testing recommended given BRCA2 variant of unknown significance identified (p.D2723H, AF 48%, likely germline). Note: EGFR amplification and CCND1 amplification are co-occurring alterations commonly seen in HPV-positive OPSCC; the absence of TP53 mutation is consistent with HPV-driven oncogenesis.

At age 52 years, the patient was seen in multidisciplinary tumor board (head and neck oncology, radiation oncology, speech-language pathology, dietetics, social work). The primary recommendation was definitive concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin-based chemotherapy and intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), given T2N1M0 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker. Surgical resection (transoral robotic surgery, TORS) was discussed but the tumor board recommended against upfront surgery given the nodal involvement and the established efficacy of definitive CRT in this setting. De-escalation trials (ECOG 3311, NRG HN002) were discussed but the patient was not enrolled. The patient was counseled extensively on acute toxicities including mucositis, xerostomia, dysphagia, and the need for potential nutritional support. Baseline FEES (functional endoscopic evaluation of swallowing): mild pharyngeal phase delay, no aspiration on thin liquids. Baseline audiogram: normal bilateral hearing thresholds. Baseline creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, GFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2. Dental evaluation completed: no extractions required, dental clearance obtained. PEG tube placement discussed prophylactically given concern for nutritional compromise during treatment. Patient consented to treatment. Cancer present.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement by interventional gastroenterology, in anticipation of nutritional compromise during concurrent chemoradiation. The procedure was uncomplicated. The patient was counseled by dietetics on PEG tube management, caloric requirements (~2200 kcal/day during treatment), and transitional feeding strategies. The plan was for PEG use on an as-needed basis with preference for continued oral intake as tolerated.

At age 52 years, the patient began definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. Treatment plan: high-risk CTV (primary tumor + right level IIA node) received 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction); intermediate-risk CTV (right neck levels II-IV, bilateral retropharyngeal nodes) received 63 Gy in 35 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction); low-risk CTV (bilateral neck levels II-IV elective) received 56 Gy in 35 fractions (1.6 Gy/fraction). Sparing structures: mean dose to contralateral parotid 22 Gy (goal <26 Gy achieved), mean spinal cord max <45 Gy, mandible max <70 Gy. Treatment delivered 5 days per week over 7 weeks. Setup: thermoplastic immobilization mask, daily CBCT image guidance. IMRT delivered using VMAT (volumetric modulated arc therapy) technique with 2 arcs. Total treatment duration: 7 weeks. No treatment interruptions. Concurrent with weekly cisplatin chemotherapy.

At age 52 years, the patient received concurrent cisplatin chemotherapy administered weekly during IMRT at a dose of 40 mg/m2 IV (body surface area 1.72 m2, dose 69 mg IV weekly) with standard pre-hydration (1 L normal saline over 1 hour pre-cisplatin) and post-hydration (1 L normal saline over 2 hours post-cisplatin), ondansetron 24 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, and magnesium sulfate 2 g IV as antiemetic prophylaxis. Amifostine was not used. The patient received 6 of 7 planned weekly cycles; week 5 dose was held due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine rise to 1.3 mg/dL) and grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 88 x10^9/L). Cumulative cisplatin dose delivered: 414 mg/m2 (83% of planned dose). This was considered adequate systemic therapy.

At age 52 years, during week 4 of concurrent chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 3 oral mucositis (confluent pseudomembranous mucositis involving the base of tongue, bilateral oral mucosa, and oropharynx) per CTCAE v5.0 criteria. She reported severe odynophagia (pain score 9/10), inability to swallow thin liquids, and significant weight loss of 6 kg (8% body weight). She was admitted to the hospital for 3 days for IV hydration, electrolyte repletion (magnesium 2.5 mg/dL, repleted with IV mag sulfate), and pain management (initiation of patient-controlled analgesia with morphine, transitioned to extended-release oxycodone with immediate-release oxycodone for breakthrough). PEG tube feeds were initiated at this time with goal rate of Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr for 16 hours per day (1800 kcal/day via tube). The patient was discharged with close outpatient follow-up and was able to complete the radiation treatment without further interruptions. Oral mucositis management included magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine/lidocaine/Maalox), oral cryotherapy, and dexamethasone oral rinse.

At age 52 years, concurrent with radiation treatment, the patient also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis (moist desquamation of right neck skin in radiation field), grade 2 dysgeusia (significant taste alteration), grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue. Dermatitis was managed with barrier cream (Mepitel film, aquaphor). Grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, baseline 0.8 mg/dL) developed during week 5 of treatment, necessitating a 1-week cisplatin hold and aggressive IV hydration. Creatinine returned to 1.0 mg/dL after hydration. No ototoxicity noted on monitoring audiogram performed after 4th cisplatin cycle. No peripheral neuropathy developed.

At age 52 years, immediately following completion of concurrent chemoradiation, the patient was seen in oncology and radiation follow-up. She had lost a total of 9 kg (12% body weight) during treatment and was fully dependent on PEG tube nutrition. Physical examination showed resolving mucositis (now grade 1-2, improving). Right neck palpation: previously palpable right level II node now no longer palpable. ECOG performance status: 2 (due to fatigue, pain, and dysphagia). She was continued on PEG tube feeds and initiated aggressive swallowing rehabilitation with speech-language pathology (daily exercises, thermal-tactile stimulation). Plan was for restaging imaging at 8-12 weeks post-treatment per NCCN guidelines. Cancer status: indeterminate (too early post-treatment for assessment).

At age 52 years, approximately 10 weeks after completion of definitive chemoradiation, the patient underwent restaging PET-CT. Results: Complete metabolic response. No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 2.1, consistent with post-treatment changes/physiologic activity). Previously FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node: now resolved, replaced by a 0.8 cm fibrotic-appearing node with no FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.8). No new areas of FDG-avid disease. No distant metastases. Impression: Complete metabolic response to concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive right base of tongue SCC. No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. Residual inflammatory changes in pharyngeal mucosa consistent with post-radiation changes.

At age 52 years, approximately 10 weeks after completion of chemoradiation, MRI of the neck with and without contrast was also obtained for anatomic correlation. MRI showed significant decrease in the right base of tongue mass: now measuring 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement and T2 signal, representing post-treatment fibrosis/scar vs. minimal residual tumor. Right level IIA lymph node now 0.8 cm with no internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy. No new lesions. Impression: Significant radiographic response, complete or near-complete, at the primary site and regional nodes. Consistent with PET-CT findings of complete metabolic response. No evidence of distant metastases.

At age 52 years (approximately 3 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in multidisciplinary follow-up. PET-CT and MRI confirmed complete metabolic response. Physical examination: oropharynx with post-radiation changes (mucositis resolved, now with visible fibrotic changes at right base of tongue), no palpable neck adenopathy. Swallowing rehabilitation progressing: patient able to tolerate pureed diet orally, PEG tube use decreased to approximately 50% of caloric needs. Weight stable at 67 kg (9 kg below pre-treatment weight). Creatinine normalized to 0.9 mg/dL. The tumor board discussed the need for adjuvant therapy: given complete response on PET-CT imaging and HPV-positive histology, no adjuvant systemic therapy or surgery was recommended. Surveillance schedule planned per NCCN HPV-positive OPSCC guidelines. De-escalation discussion: the patient was offered enrollment in a post-treatment surveillance de-escalation protocol but declined. Cancer absent (complete response). No evidence of disease.

At age 52 years (approximately 5 months post-treatment), the patient underwent repeat direct laryngoscopy and biopsy of the right base of tongue region of concern identified on MRI (residual 0.9 cm lesion). This was performed under general anesthesia with narrow-band imaging (NBI). Findings: smooth post-radiation mucosa at the right base of tongue with no visible tumor; the 0.9 cm MRI lesion appeared to represent fibrotic tissue. Random biopsies from the base of tongue, right lateral pharyngeal wall, right tonsil fossa (residual cuff), and hypopharynx were all taken. Pathology pending.

At age 52 years (approximately 5 months post-treatment), pathology from direct laryngoscopy biopsies of the right base of tongue and oropharynx returned as follows: Right base of tongue (3 fragments): extensive radiation changes with stromal fibrosis, vascular ectasia, and atypical stromal cells consistent with radiation effect. No viable carcinoma identified. Right lateral pharyngeal wall (2 fragments): squamous mucosa with reactive changes, no malignancy. Right tonsillar fossa (2 fragments): benign lymphoid tissue with post-radiation fibrosis, no malignancy. Hypopharynx (2 fragments): squamous mucosa, no malignancy. Conclusion: No viable squamous cell carcinoma identified. Findings consistent with complete pathologic response to definitive chemoradiation.

At age 52 years (approximately 6 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology follow-up. Pathology from direct laryngoscopy confirmed complete pathologic response - no viable tumor. The patient was counseled that this represents a complete response and that the plan would be ongoing surveillance. Swallowing status: patient now tolerating soft mechanical diet with minimal PEG use (<25% caloric needs). She was hoping to have the PEG tube removed. Xerostomia: grade 1-2 persistent, managed with frequent sips of water, Biotene, salivary substitutes. Voice quality: mild hoarseness, stable. Weight: 68 kg, slowly recovering. ECOG PS: 1. Thyroid function: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (elevated, consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism) - levothyroxine dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Plan: continue close surveillance, PEG removal in 4-6 weeks if oral intake adequate. Cancer absent.

At age 52 years (approximately 7 months post-treatment), the patient underwent uncomplicated PEG tube removal by gastroenterology after demonstrating adequate oral caloric intake (>1800 kcal/day orally, weight stable at 69 kg). The PEG site healed without complication. The patient was transitioned to full oral diet (soft mechanical) and continued with speech-language pathology for swallowing rehabilitation targeting advancement to regular diet consistency.

At age 52 years (approximately 9 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent surveillance PET-CT as part of the 9-12 month post-treatment imaging protocol. Results: No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 1.9, consistent with post-treatment changes). No pathologic cervical adenopathy. No distant metastases identified. Impression: No evidence of recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Complete ongoing metabolic response. Stable post-treatment changes in the oropharynx.

At age 53 years (approximately 12 months post-treatment), the patient was evaluated for late radiation toxicities. She reported persistent grade 2 xerostomia (significantly decreased salivary production, dry mouth affecting sleep and diet), grade 1-2 dysphagia (able to manage modified regular diet but avoids dry foods and thick solids), and grade 1 trismus (mouth opening 3.8 cm, baseline estimated 4.5 cm). No radiation-induced carotid stenosis on ultrasound screening. No osteonecrosis of the mandible. Audiogram: high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (4000-8000 Hz bilaterally), grade 1, likely related to cisplatin. Managed with Biotene, pilocarpine 5 mg TID (initiated for xerostomia with modest benefit), ongoing jaw stretching exercises with TheraBite device, and quarterly speech-language pathology sessions. TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normalized on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily). No features of radiation-induced fibrosis or lymphedema of the neck.

At age 53 years (12 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in annual follow-up in the head and neck survivorship clinic. She reported significantly improved quality of life compared to 6 months post-treatment. She had returned to work as a music teacher at 10 months post-treatment. She was tolerating a regular-modified diet, had regained 3 kg (now 71 kg), and was managing xerostomia with regular pilocarpine and salivary substitutes. Physical examination: no palpable cervical adenopathy, no oral mucosal lesions, oropharynx with post-radiation mucosal changes but no visible tumor. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: post-radiation changes at right base of tongue (smooth, fibrotic mucosa), no visible lesion. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 12 months. Continued surveillance scheduled per NCCN guidelines (clinical exam every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, annually thereafter).

At age 53 years (18 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent MRI of the neck as part of surveillance (18-month imaging checkpoint). Results: Stable post-treatment changes at the right base of tongue. The previously noted 0.9 cm area of fibrosis has decreased to 0.5 cm and demonstrates no enhancement on post-contrast sequences. No new mucosal lesions. Right level IIA node: stable 0.7 cm fibrotic-appearing node, no internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy in the neck. No evidence of local recurrence or new distant metastases. Impression: No radiographic evidence of recurrent disease. Stable/improving post-treatment changes consistent with ongoing fibrosis and resolution.

At age 53 years (18 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology surveillance. MRI stable. No clinical concerns. Physical examination: no palpable adenopathy, flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows stable post-radiation changes, no visible lesion. TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (euthyroid on levothyroxine 100 mcg). She reported continued improvement in swallowing; now tolerating regular diet with only occasional avoidance of dry or sticky foods. Xerostomia: persistent grade 1-2 but manageable. Trismus: improved to 4.1 cm mouth opening with TheraBite exercises. Audiogram: stable, mild high-frequency SNHL bilaterally (grade 1). Pilocarpine continued. No signs of recurrence. Cancer absent at 18 months. Plan: transition to less frequent follow-up (every 4-6 months). Annual thyroid ultrasound initiated (radiation-induced thyroid cancer screening). Discussion of late effects monitoring including carotid artery disease screening given radiation exposure to neck vasculature.

At age 54 years (24 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent surveillance PET-CT at the 2-year mark. Results: No evidence of FDG-avid disease at the primary site (right base of tongue) or regional nodes. SUVmax at base of tongue: 1.7 (well within physiologic range). No pathologic adenopathy. No distant metastases. Thyroid: no FDG-avid thyroid nodules. Impression: Complete ongoing metabolic response, no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease at 24 months post-treatment. Excellent prognosis given HPV-positive histology and sustained complete response.

At age 54 years (24 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology follow-up. PET-CT confirmed no evidence of disease at 2 years. This milestone is highly significant for HPV-positive OPSCC, as the majority of late recurrences (>24 months post-treatment) are uncommon in this biology. The patient was counseled that her 5-year locoregional control and overall survival rates are estimated at 85-90% given complete response, HPV-positive status, and never-smoker profile. She was informed that surveillance imaging frequency would be reduced going forward. De-escalation of surveillance to annual imaging and clinical exam every 6 months was discussed and implemented. The patient was also referred to genetics counseling for the germline BRCA2 variant of unknown significance identified on NGS. Quality of life: returned to full professional duties, active lifestyle, minimal limitations. Xerostomia: grade 1, stable. No trismus. No lymphedema. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent at 24 months. No evidence of disease.

At age 54 years (30 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in routine survivorship follow-up. No new symptoms. Physical examination unremarkable, no palpable lymphadenopathy, flexible nasopharyngoscopy with no mucosal lesions or visible tumor. Thyroid ultrasound: no thyroid nodules. Carotid artery Doppler ultrasound (first screening, 30 months post-neck irradiation): mild intimal-medial thickening of right common carotid artery at the radiation field, no hemodynamically significant stenosis, <30% stenosis bilaterally. Lipid panel: LDL 118 mg/dL, initiated low-dose statin (atorvastatin 10 mg) per cardiovascular risk reduction guidelines for radiation-exposed carotid arteries. Audiology: stable mild high-frequency SNHL bilaterally (grade 1, unchanged). Pilocarpine dose tapered to 5 mg BID. Genetics: BRCA2 p.D2723H variant reclassified as likely benign by genetics counseling team, no additional BRCA testing warranted for family members at this time. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent at 30 months.

scenario_blurb: HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (base of tongue) stage III (T2N1M0) in a 52-year-old nonsmoker. Treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin and intensity-modulated radiation therapy. Develops grade 3 mucositis and requires PEG tube for nutrition support. Achieves complete response. Post-treatment surveillance with consideration of de-escalation in the adjuvant setting.
